content:
# Leviticus Chapter 9 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)

**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 62v–63r | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus pp. 93–94

---

**1** On the eighth day, Moses called for Aaron and his sons and the elders of Israel. **2** He said to Aaron, "Take a young bull for your sin offering and a ram for your burnt offering — both without defect — and present them before the LORD. **3** Then say to the Israelites: Take a male goat for your sin offering, and a calf and a lamb — yearlings without defect — for your burnt offering, **4** and an ox and a ram for your peace offerings, to sacrifice before the LORD. Also bring a grain offering mixed with oil. For today the LORD will appear to you."

**5** They brought everything Moses had commanded to the front of the tent of meeting, and the whole community came near and stood before the LORD. **6** Moses said, "This is what the LORD has commanded you to do, so that the glory of the LORD may appear to you."

**7** Moses said to Aaron, "Come to the altar and present your sin offering and your burnt offering to make atonement for yourself and for the people. Then present the people's offering to make atonement for them, just as the LORD has commanded."

**8** Aaron came to the altar and slaughtered the calf as his own sin offering. **9** His sons brought the blood to him, and he dipped his finger in the blood and put it on the horns of the altar, pouring out the rest of the blood at the base of the altar. **10** The fat, the kidneys, and the lobe of the liver from the sin offering he burned on the altar, just as the LORD had commanded Moses. **11** The flesh and the hide he burned in the fire outside the camp.

**12** Then he slaughtered the burnt offering. His sons handed him the blood, and he threw it against all sides of the altar. **13** They handed him the burnt offering piece by piece, including the head, and he burned them on the altar. **14** He washed the inner organs and the legs and burned them on top of the burnt offering on the altar.

**15** Then he presented the people's offering. He took the goat for the people's sin offering, slaughtered it, and offered it for sin as he had done with the first. **16** He presented the burnt offering and offered it according to the prescribed manner. **17** He presented the grain offering, took a handful of it, and burned it on the altar — in addition to the morning's burnt offering.

**18** He slaughtered the ox and the ram as the people's peace offerings. His sons handed him the blood, and he threw it against all sides of the altar. **19** The fat portions of the ox and the ram — the fat tail, the layer of fat, the kidneys, and the lobe of the liver — **20** they placed on top of the breasts, and he burned the fat portions on the altar. **21** Aaron waved the breasts and the right thigh before the LORD as a wave offering — just as Moses had commanded.

**22** Aaron lifted his hands toward the people and blessed them. Then he stepped down from presenting the sin offering, the burnt offering, and the peace offerings.

**23** Moses and Aaron went into the tent of meeting. When they came out, they blessed the people — and the glory of the LORD appeared to all the people.

**24** Fire came out from before the LORD and consumed the burnt offering and the fat portions on the altar. When all the people saw it, they shouted and fell facedown.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**The eighth day — beyond the seven.** The entire chapter turns on the significance of the number eight. For seven days Aaron and his sons have remained at the entrance of the tent of meeting, enclosed in the liminal space of ordination, eating the ordination food, being formed for their office. Seven is the number of completion in Israel's symbolic world: the seven days of creation, the seven days of any major purification or consecration period. To reach the eighth day is to step beyond completion — into a new beginning on the other side of the completed formation. The eighth day is not merely the day after the seven; it is the first day of a new order. Aaron does not simply emerge from training and go to work. He emerges from a seven-day transformation into a new mode of existence — as Israel's consecrated high priest — and the first act of that new existence is precisely the act he was formed to perform: the offering of sacrifice before the LORD. The eighth day is new creation language. It speaks of something that cannot be reached by human effort alone, only by passing through the full ordained sequence and being met on the other side by God.

**"The LORD will appear to you" — the stakes of the chapter.** Verse 4 announces what is at stake in this entire ceremony: "For today the LORD will appear to you." And verse 6 amplifies it: "This is what the LORD has commanded you to do, so that the glory of the LORD may appear to you." The entire elaborate structure of offerings — the sin offering and burnt offering for Aaron, the sin offering and burnt offering and grain offering and peace offerings for the people — is not an end in itself. It is a prepared space for divine self-disclosure. The offerings create the conditions under which the Holy One can appear in the midst of an unholy people without destroying them. Atonement is not merely moral accounting; it is the precondition for encounter. The sin offering clears the way; the burnt offering consecrates the meeting; the peace offering celebrates the communion that has become possible. What all of it is aimed at — what the entire priestly system exists to enable — is the moment when God comes near and the people see glory.

This is Leviticus's answer to the question every reader of Exodus has been asking since the tent was constructed: will the LORD actually dwell among them? Chapter 8 performed the ordination. Chapter 9 lights the flame. The question is answered not in words but in fire.

**Aaron's own sin offering — the priest's necessary self-atonement.** Verse 7 specifies the sequence: "Present your sin offering and your burnt offering to make atonement for yourself and for the people." Aaron must first atone for himself before he can atone for Israel. This is a structural feature of the Aaronic priesthood that the book of Hebrews will explicitly contrast with the priesthood of Christ: "Unlike the other high priests, he does not need to offer sacrifices day after day, first for his own sins, and then for the sins of the people" (Hebrews 7:27). Aaron's priesthood requires a prior self-reckoning. He is not a sinless mediator but a forgiven one — a man who stands before the LORD only because the blood of the calf has already been applied on his behalf. The high priest of Israel intercedes for the people from within the people, carrying his own need alongside theirs. There is something humbling and human about this. The one who will receive Israel's confessions and offer Israel's sacrifices is himself a man who needs atonement. He mediates not from a position of innate moral superiority but from a position of shared human frailty, covered by the same grace he extends to others.

**The double structure — Aaron first, then the people.** Verses 8–14 deal with Aaron's own offerings; verses 15–21 deal with the people's offerings. The doubling is deliberate. The priest does not skip his own accountability in order to serve others more efficiently. He cannot. The order is fixed by divine instruction: self-atonement precedes intercession. This sequence speaks to a universal truth about spiritual leadership that Leviticus encodes in liturgical form: the one who ministers to others must first stand under the same verdict as those they serve. The pastor who preaches forgiveness must herself receive forgiveness. The priest who lifts the people's burdens must first lay down his own. There is no bypass route to effective intercession; the road runs through the altar, and the altar requires the priest's own blood-costly reckoning.

**Peace offerings and the logic of wholeness.** Verse 4 specifies that the people are to bring an ox and a ram for their *peace offerings* — *shelamim*, from the root *shalom*. The peace offering is the one sacrificial category in which the offerer participates in eating the sacrifice: portions are burned on the altar for the LORD, portions go to the priests, and portions are eaten by the offerer and family in a sacred communal meal. The peace offering is therefore not merely a gesture of propitiation but a celebration of restored relationship — the human being and God sharing a meal, so to speak, at the same table. The Hebrew *shalom* does not mean merely "peace" in the sense of absence of conflict; it means completeness, wholeness, flourishing — the full integration of the parts into a harmonious whole. The peace offering enacts, in symbolic ritual space, the condition that the sin offering has made possible: the sinner, forgiven, at table with the Holy One. Verse 4 announces these peace offerings before the atonement has happened — as a statement of intent and anticipated joy. The ceremony begins with the expectation of wholeness. That expectation is answered in fire at the end.

**Aaron blesses the people — the hands of the priest.** Verse 22 is one of the most quietly charged moments in Leviticus: "Aaron lifted his hands toward the people and blessed them." After all the blood and fat and burning, after the meticulous execution of offering after offering, the priest turns to face the people — not the altar — and lifts his hands in blessing. This is not an afterthought. It is the completion of the priestly function. The priest mediates between the people and the LORD, and the mediation runs in both directions: the people's offerings go up toward God through the priest's actions at the altar; God's blessing comes down toward the people through the priest's raised hands. The Aaronic Blessing of Numbers 6:24–26 ("The LORD bless you and keep you...") is the verbal content that fills the gesture of verse 22. Aaron's raised hands are the physical embodiment of his office: he is the conduit, the living connection point between the people's need and the LORD's abundance.

The gesture of raised hands in blessing will travel the entire length of the biblical story. Melchizedek blesses Abraham. Moses raises his hands at the Amalekite battle. The returning Christ in Luke 24 lifts his hands and blesses his disciples even as he is taken up. The priest's raised hands are one of Scripture's oldest and most persistent images of divine grace flowing through human instrumentality.

**Moses and Aaron enter the tent together — the unique moment.** Verse 23 records something extraordinary: "Moses and Aaron went into the tent of meeting." This is the only place in the entire Pentateuch where both Moses and Aaron enter the tent together. Moses has entered alone throughout Exodus and Leviticus, receiving divine speech at the mercy seat. Aaron will enter alone on the Day of Atonement (Leviticus 16), with all the elaborate precautions for approaching the holy of holies. But here — on the eighth day, the day of inauguration — they go in together. No explanation is given of what happens inside. The text is silent about the content of their encounter. But they come out together and bless the people together, and immediately afterward the glory of the LORD appears. Something decisive has occurred in that shared moment of entry. The transition of authority that has been unfolding across chapters 8–9 reaches its pivot point inside the tent: Moses, who has been the sole divine interlocutor, now stands alongside Aaron, who is beginning to occupy the office he has been ordained for. They bless the people with one voice. The era of priestly mediation has begun.

**The fire from before the LORD — the climax of the book so far.** Verse 24 is the theological summit of everything from Exodus 25 onward: "Fire came out from before the LORD and consumed the burnt offering and the fat portions on the altar." The construction of the tabernacle (Exodus 25–40), the consecration of its contents (Leviticus 8), the first official priestly service (Leviticus 9) — all of it has been moving toward this moment. God himself acts. The fire does not come from the priests, from their preparation, from the perfection of their technique. It comes from before the LORD — from the divine presence itself. The offerings are accepted not by human certification but by divine combustion. The LORD's fire is the ultimate validation: this is pleasing, this is received, this is the kind of worship I have asked for and I now accept.

The fire of verse 24 is creation language echoing forward: the same God who spoke light into being now speaks fire into the altar. The God who breathed life into Adam now breathes flame into the sacrifice. The offerings become, through that divine fire, a *pleasing aroma* — the priest cannot make them so by technique alone. Only the fire of the LORD's own presence transforms a dead animal on a stone structure into an act of worship.

**"They shouted and fell facedown."** The chapter's final image — the people shouting and falling prostrate — is the instinctive human response to divine self-disclosure. The two responses are simultaneous: vocal exaltation and physical abasement. They cry out in joy or astonishment (*va-yaronnu*), and they collapse (*va-yiplu*) on their faces. Both responses are right. Both are called for. Leviticus does not allow the people to maintain composed dignity in the presence of the LORD's glory. The fire that comes from before the LORD is too much, too real, too consuming for the spectator to remain standing. And the shout is not a polished liturgical response but a raw, involuntary cry — the sound that escapes when the holy thing happens and there are no adequate words for it.

This ending answers Exodus 40:38, where the cloud of the LORD was over the tabernacle by day and fire was in the cloud by night — and the Israelites could see it throughout their journeys. Now the fire is not just in the cloud above; it has descended to the altar below. Heaven and earth have touched in the space of the sacrifice. The people are witnesses. The only fitting responses are a shout and a fall.

**LXX (Vaticanus) observations — pages 93–94.** Codex Vaticanus presents Leviticus 9 in its characteristic careful uncial hand. Several readings illuminate the Greek tradition's interpretation.

At verse 4, the LXX renders the announcement "the LORD will appear to you" (*ki ha-yom YHWH nir'eh aleikhem*) as *ὅτι σήμερον κύριος ὀφθήσεται ὑμῖν* — "because today the Lord will be seen by you." The Greek verb *ōphthēsetai* (future passive of *horaō*) is the same form used throughout the LXX theophanies — God "appeared" to Abraham, to Moses, to the patriarchs. The choice of *ōphthēsetai* places this promised appearance squarely within the great sequence of divine self-disclosures that form the backbone of the Pentateuch's theological narrative. Today's appearance to the assembled community is of the same order as the appearances to the individual patriarchs — perhaps greater, because it is corporate. What was given to Abraham alone is now being given to the whole people.

At verse 7, the LXX's rendering of "make atonement for yourself and for the people" uses *ἐξίλασαι περὶ σεαυτοῦ καὶ περὶ τοῦ οἴκου σου* in some traditions — "for yourself and for your household" — following a variant reading, though Vaticanus follows the MT more closely with the people (*laos*) rather than household (*oikos*). The Vaticanus reading aligns the text with its function: Aaron is not atoning for a private family unit but for the covenant community as a whole. The priest's intercession is inherently corporate; his family is subsumed into his public priestly office.

At verse 22, the LXX's description of Aaron's blessing (*ἐπάρας τὰς χεῖρας αὐτοῦ ἐπὶ τὸν λαόν*, "lifting his hands over the people") uses *epārās* — the aorist of *epairō*, to lift or raise. This specific verb for lifting hands in blessing will echo through the LXX psalms and into the New Testament. Luke 24:50 uses the identical construction when the risen Christ blesses his disciples: *ἐπάρας τὰς χεῖρας αὐτοῦ εὐλόγησεν αὐτούς*. The verbal link is unmistakable, and almost certainly deliberate for a Greek-speaking early Christian reader. The ascension scene in Luke directly evokes the Aaronic blessing of Leviticus 9: the high priest lifts his hands over the people and blesses them as he withdraws from their sight. Jesus, the great High Priest, performs the identical gesture at the moment of his departure from the disciples. He leaves them not with a final teaching but with a blessing — hands raised, in the gesture Aaron first performed on the eighth day.

At verse 24, the LXX's description of the divine fire (*ἐξῆλθεν πῦρ παρὰ κυρίου* — "fire went out from beside/before the Lord") uses *para kyriou* rather than the more common *apo kyriou* — "from beside the Lord" rather than simply "from the Lord." The preposition *para* (beside, from the side of) localizes the fire as coming from the immediate presence, from the very vicinity of the LORD's dwelling in the tent. This is not fire falling from heaven; it is fire erupting from the place where the LORD has taken up residence. The tent of meeting is not merely a symbol of divine presence; it is the actual locus of that presence, and the fire that validates the worship comes from within the place where God dwells. The LXX's *para* captures the intimacy and the immediacy of the divine act: this is fire from within the holy precincts, from beside the One who inhabits the most holy place.

The people's response in the LXX — *ἀνεβόησαν καὶ ἔπεσον ἐπὶ πρόσωπον* — renders their shout with *aneboēsan*, the aorist of *anaboaō*, a verb used for cries of intense distress or astonishment. It is not a word for polite liturgical response. It is the word for involuntary outcry — the shout that escapes when something too large for ordinary response breaks through the surface of human experience. And then the fall: *ἔπεσον ἐπὶ πρόσωπον* — "they fell upon the face" — the prostration that is both worship and overwhelm. In the LXX, this combination of cry and prostration echoes the response of Israel at Mount Sinai (Exodus 19–20) and anticipates the prostrations before the divine throne in Ezekiel and Revelation. The fire from before the LORD in Leviticus 9 is a Sinai-event compressed into a single altar moment — the holy mountain has descended into the holy tent, and the people respond as they responded on the mountain: in awe, in fall, in wordless cry.

---

*PNEUMA — translated from Leningrad Codex with reference to Codex Vaticanus (LXX).*
